sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: TJX is led publicly by Executive Chairman Carol Meyrowitz and Chief Executive Officer and President Ernie Herrman, with the company operating a large, multi-banner off-price model. Fiscal 2026 results show execution at scale: net sales reached $60.4 billion, consolidated comparable sales rose 5%, and the company ended the year with over 5,200 stores across four segments.
points: Carol Meyrowitz signed the 2026 proxy letter as Executive Chairman of the Board, while Ernie Herrman signed as Chief Executive Officer and President.
Management runs a broad off-price platform across Marmaxx, HomeGoods, TJX Canada, and TJX International, with banners spanning the U.S., Canada, Europe, and Australia.
Fiscal 2026 execution included $60.4 billion of net sales, the first year TJX said annual sales surpassed $60 billion, and strong comparable sales increases at each division.
The operating model requires coordinated buying, logistics, and store execution across approximately 377,000 employees and more than 5,200 stores at fiscal 2026 year-end.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: TJX balanced growth investment, shareholder returns, and liquidity in fiscal 2026. The company added 129 net new stores, remodeled almost 500 stores, returned $4.3 billion to shareholders, and ended the year with $5.335 billion of cash and equivalents against $2.866 billion of long-term debt.
points: TJX added 129 net new stores and remodeled almost 500 stores during fiscal 2026, continuing to invest in its physical off-price retail footprint.
The company returned $4.3 billion to shareholders in fiscal 2026 while also reporting $6.057 billion of operating cash flow and $4.474 billion of net income.
TJX ended FY2026 with $5.335 billion of cash and equivalents, $6.364 billion of stockholders' equity, and $2.866 billion of long-term debt.
As of January 31, 2026, TJX had two revolving credit facilities totaling $1.5 billion, with no amounts outstanding and compliance with all related covenants.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: The strongest shareholder-alignment evidence in the packet is financial: TJX returned $4.3 billion to shareholders in fiscal 2026 while continuing store growth and remodeling. The packet does not provide executive ownership or compensation-plan details, so alignment is best judged from capital returns, balance sheet discipline, and disclosed operating performance rather than pay structure.
points: Fiscal 2026 shareholder returns were material at $4.3 billion, supported by $6.057 billion of operating cash flow and $4.474 billion of net income.
TJX continued reinvesting in the business while returning capital, adding 129 net new stores and remodeling almost 500 stores during the fiscal year.
The balance sheet included $5.335 billion of cash and equivalents and $2.866 billion of long-term debt at FY2026 year-end, supporting continued financial flexibility.
TJX also carried $10.620 billion of operating lease liabilities as of January 31, 2026, a key obligation shareholders should weigh against the store-based model.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: TJX provides investors with segment, banner, operating, and financial disclosures through its proxy statement, 10-K, and SEC company facts. The packet includes concrete reporting on four segments, store count, country footprint, comparable sales, net sales, shareholder returns, cash flow, debt, leases, taxes, and risk factors.
points: TJX identifies four reportable segments: Marmaxx, HomeGoods, TJX Canada, and TJX International, and discloses major banners within each geography.
The company reports operating metrics such as more than 5,200 stores, 129 net new stores, almost 500 remodels, and stores in 9 countries across three continents.
Fiscal 2026 investor disclosures included $60.4 billion of net sales, 5% consolidated comparable sales growth, $4.474 billion of net income, and $6.057 billion of operating cash flow.
The 10-K disclosed financing details, including five series of senior unsecured notes, $2.878 billion of total debt, $1.5 billion of unused revolving credit capacity, and lease liabilities.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Governance disclosures in the packet show a standard public-company framework with annual director elections, an external auditor vote, and named enterprise risk categories. The packet supports board and audit oversight facts, but it does not include details on director independence, insider ownership, or executive compensation structure.
points: TJX nominated ten directors for election at the 2026 Annual Meeting, with elected nominees expected to serve until the 2027 Annual Meeting.
Shareholders were asked to ratify PricewaterhouseCoopers as TJX's independent registered public accounting firm for fiscal 2027.

The proxy identified company-specific risks including buying strategy, inventory management, merchandise sourcing and transport, tariffs, data security, labor costs, currency exchange, litigation, taxes, and indebtedness.
TJX's store model creates governance-relevant oversight needs because virtually all store locations are leased and total operating lease liabilities were $10.620 billion as of January 31, 2026.

types.type: Scale in off-price retail sourcing and distribution
types.summary: TJX's scale is central to its off-price model: it operated over 5,200 stores, generated $60.4 billion of fiscal 2026 net sales, and employed approximately 377,000 Associates. That footprint supports opportunistic buying across department store cancellations, manufacturer overruns, and closeouts from brands and other retailers.

TJX offers brand-name and designer merchandise at prices generally 20% to 60% below full-price retailers' regular prices on comparable merchandise. Its stores receive fresh merchandise regularly, creating a treasure-hunt shopping experience designed to encourage frequent visits and cross-shopping across banners.

TJX stores are built for changing assortments, with adaptable layouts, few permanent fixtures, and no walls between departments. The company also tailors merchandise to local preferences and customer demographics while seeking rapid inventory turnover across a wide array of products.

TJX operates multiple banners across apparel and home fashions, including TJ Maxx, Marshalls, HomeGoods, Homesense, Winners, TK Maxx, and Sierra. At fiscal 2026 year-end, the company had stores in 9 countries across three continents, giving the model several formats and geographies for growth.

types.type: Logistics model built around frequent merchandise flow
types.summary: TJX says its logistics and distribution operations are designed to support quick, efficient, and differentiated delivery across a geographically diverse store network. This matters because the off-price model depends on regular merchandise flow, local assortment decisions, and rapid inventory turnover rather than fixed seasonal assortments.
